content:
Here is a quick summary of the search.  One shows first that if none of \(a,b,c\) is 1 then by bounding divisors the only possible “big” solution up to symmetry is
\[
(a,b,c)=(2,3,7)\,,
\]
and one checks that indeed
\[
2\cdot3\cdot7=42
\quad\Big|\quad
(2\cdot3+1)(3\cdot7+1)(7\cdot2+1)=7\cdot22\cdot15=2310.
\]
Next one checks the case “exactly one of \(a,b,c\) is 1” forces \(\{\,a,b,c\}=\{1,2,3\}\), giving \(3!\!=6\) ordered triples, and “exactly two of them are 1” forces the triple \((1,1,2)\) up to permutation, giving \(3\) more, plus the trivial \((1,1,1)\).  Altogether the ordered solutions are

• (1,1,1) → 1  
• permutations of (1,1,2) → 3  
• permutations of (1,2,3) → 6  
• permutations of (2,3,7) → 6  

Total = \(1+3+6+6=16\).  

Answer: \(\boxed{16}\).

message.content:
Here is a quick summary of the search.  One shows first that if none of \(a,b,c\) is 1 then by bounding divisors the only possible “big” solution up to symmetry is
\[
(a,b,c)=(2,3,7)\,,
\]
and one checks that indeed
\[
2\cdot3\cdot7=42
\quad\Big|\quad
(2\cdot3+1)(3\cdot7+1)(7\cdot2+1)=7\cdot22\cdot15=2310.
\]
Next one checks the case “exactly one of \(a,b,c\) is 1” forces \(\{\,a,b,c\}=\{1,2,3\}\), giving \(3!\!=6\) ordered triples, and “exactly two of them are 1” forces the triple \((1,1,2)\) up to permutation, giving \(3\) more, plus the trivial \((1,1,1)\).  Altogether the ordered solutions are

• (1,1,1) → 1  
• permutations of (1,1,2) → 3  
• permutations of (1,2,3) → 6  
• permutations of (2,3,7) → 6  

Total = \(1+3+6+6=16\).  

Answer: \(\boxed{16}\).
